coredata.dc:title: Low temperature synthesis of chiral carbon dots for reducing H2O2 damage
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Beibei
Wang, Yuying
Zhang, Xiaoyang
Liu, Yi
coredata.dc:description: Recently, researches focused towards the chiral nanostructures have attracted vast attention. However, the synthesis of chiral carbon dots (CDs) through one-step method is still rather scarce. Herein, a universal approach to green synthesis of chiral CDs at low temperature was proposed. In brief, L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs were obtained by only heating the fructose and chiral cysteine molecules in the sodium hydroxide aqueous solution under atmospheric pressure. Circular dichroism spectra show that these prepared CDs exhibit opposite chirality ranging from 210 to 260 nm. Specially, the prepared L-FruCDs could reduce the intracellular oxidative damage induced by hydrogen peroxide and display a superior performance than that of D-FruCDs. Mechanism studies indicate that the probably protect mechanism is ascribed to the directly consumption the intracellular ROS. And the clearance efficiency of intracellular reactive oxygen species of L-FruCDs is 3-times than that of D-FruCDs. Furthermore, this newly synthesized method is scalable by replacing fructose precursor with ascorbic acid, sucrose or lactose. In sum, our work provides a new method for the preparation of chiral CDs and achieve a great success in exploring the chiral biological effects at nanoscale.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral
Carbon dots
Antioxidative
Reducing oxidative stress

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cytotoxicity analysis of L/D-FruCDs in vitro
The abilities of L-/D-FruCDs for reducing oxidative damage
Detection of reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation
Protect effect of L-/D-FruCDs against oxidative damage
Intracellular ROS-scavenging ability and the possible mechanism
The universality test of the method

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by GDAS’ Project of Science and Technology Development (2021GDASYL-20210103010A), and National Natural Science Foundation of China (U23A2089).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme illustrating the synthesis of L-/D-FruCDs using a combination of fructose and chiral cysteine as precursors and their application in reducing oxidative stress damage induced by H2O2.

TEM images of (a) L-FruCDs and (b) D-FruCDs, the histograms of the corresponding particle size distribution (c) L-FruCDs and (d) D-FruCDs, the FT-IR spectra of L-/D-FruCDs (e), the XPS spectra of L-/D-FruCDs (f).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of L-FruCDs (red line) and D-FruCDs (blue line) dispersed in water, the circular dichroism spectrum of (b) L-/D-FruCDs and (c) L-/D-cysteine, the fluorescence emission spectrum of (d) L-FruCDs and (e) D-FruCDs under various excitation wavelength ranging from 320 nm ∼ 440 nm, (f) time-resolved fluorescence spectra of L-/D-FruCDs, insert ‘f′ shows the fluorescence image of L-FruCDs (left) and D-FruCDs (right) under UV light of 365 nm.

(a) Cytotoxicity evaluation of L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs, (b) cytoprotective effects of L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs against H2O2-induced stress, (c) intracellular ROS levels after incubating with 200 µg/mL L-/D-FruCDs detected by flow cytometry, (d) the intracellular FL intensity of DCF in cells treated with Control (H2O2 only), H2O2 +L-FruCDs, H2O2 +D-FruCDs groups respectively.

(a) Vis-absorption spectrum and fluorescence emission spectrum of L-yFruCDs (solid line) and D-yFruCDs (dash line) at the excitation of 410 nm, (b) circular dichroism spectrum of L-/D-yFruCDs, (c) TEM image of L-yFruCDs and (d) TEM image of D-yFruCDs, insert ‘a′ shows the fluorescence image of the L-yFruCDs (left) and D-yFruCDs (right) under UV light of 365 nm.

(a) The circular dichroism spectrum of L-LacCDs (red line) and D-LacCDs (blue line), the fluorescence emission spectrum of L-LacCDs (b) and D-LacCDs (c) under various excitation wavelength ranging from 320 nm ∼ 440 nm.

head.ce:title: Low temperature synthesis of chiral carbon dots for reducing H2O2 damage

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, researches focused towards the chiral nanostructures have attracted vast attention. However, the synthesis of chiral carbon dots (CDs) through one-step method is still rather scarce. Herein, a universal approach to green synthesis of chiral CDs at low temperature was proposed. In brief, L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs were obtained by only heating the fructose and chiral cysteine molecules in the sodium hydroxide aqueous solution under atmospheric pressure. Circular dichroism spectra show that these prepared CDs exhibit opposite chirality ranging from 210 to 260 nm. Specially, the prepared L-FruCDs could reduce the intracellular oxidative damage induced by hydrogen peroxide and display a superior performance than that of D-FruCDs. Mechanism studies indicate that the probably protect mechanism is ascribed to the directly consumption the intracellular ROS. And the clearance efficiency of intracellular reactive oxygen species of L-FruCDs is 3-times than that of D-FruCDs. Furthermore, this newly synthesized method is scalable by replacing fructose precursor with ascorbic acid, sucrose or lactose. In sum, our work provides a new method for the preparation of chiral CDs and achieve a great success in exploring the chiral biological effects at nanoscale.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chirality is an interesting geometric property in nature and widely existing in nucleic acid, protein and biomacromolecules [1,2]. Since Whetten and Schaaff discovered the existence of chirality at nanoscale, the studies focused towards chiral nanomaterials has become a hot branch in the field of nanotechnology [3–5]. It has been reported that the chirality of nanomaterials affects the cell uptake rate, cell metabolism and cytotoxicity [6–10]. For instance, Jiang et al. revealed the cationic gold nanoclusters modified with L-glutathione can effectively improve the biosafety and pharmacokinetics than the gold nanoclusters modified with D-glutathione [11]. Our previous studies also found that the chemodynamic-induced cytotoxicity induced by D-Cu2-x Se nanoparticles was stronger than that of L-Cu2-x Se, which was ascribed to the stronger internalization ability of D-Cu2-x Se [12].

Currently, carbon dots (CDs) are drawing considerable attention from researchers due to the bright fluorescence emission, good water solubility, excellent light stability, low toxicity, etc. [13–15]. And compared with these traditional CDs, chiral CDs possess many unique properties, which make CDs a prominent candidate for asymmetric catalysis, energy metabolism regulation and enzyme activity regulation [16,17]. Kang et al. found that maltase decorated by chiral CDs exhibited different inhibition to maltose hydrolytic rate [18]. Moreover, they also revealed that the CDs with chirality derived from chiral cysteine or tryptophan could selectivity tune the ability of the laccase [19,20]. Gao et al. synthesized chiral CDs using citric acid and L-/D-glutamine firstly and then developed a chiral CDs-glucose oxidase nanoreactors, which significantly enhance the activity of glucose oxidase. They also reported that the DGOx exhibited higher enzymatic activity than that of LGOx [21]. Zhao et al. synthesized chiral CDs using glutamic acid as precursor through hydrothermal method, and revealed that the antibacterial ability of L-CDs is higher than that of D-CDs [22].

However, although the chiral CDs is a type of charming materials, its synthesis method is still limited. In early studies, most chiral CDs were synthesized by two-step strategy, involving the preparation of achiral carbon dots and the attachment of chiral small molecules [23–25]. This synthesized strategy is relatively complicated. Thus, the one-step strategy was occurred and developed rapidly [22,26]. In one-step strategy, chiral CDs could be obtained through electrolysis method, hydrothermal and chemical oxidation method by using the chiral molecules as precursors [26,27]. Notably, these methods always need extreme reaction conditions, such as long reaction time, high temperature and high pressure. In addition, once the chiral properties of the precursor are accidentally lost in the synthesized process, chiral CDs cannot be obtained [28]. To date, green synthesis of chiral CDs remains enormous challenges. And it is of great significance to develop newly and eco-friendly method to prepare chiral CDs.

Herein, a universal approach to green synthesis of chiral CDs at low temperature was proposed. As demonstrated in Fig. 1, L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs were synthesized by simple heating fructose and L-cysteine or D-cysteine aqueous solution in an oil bath under atmospheric pressure. It is worth noting that the reaction was conducted at relatively low temperature (80 °C) compared with the hydrothermal method or pyrolysis method. Except for the common properties such as excellent stability, bright fluorescence emission and low cytotoxicity, the obtained chiral CDs possess the unique absorbed preference of polarized light at same time. In addition, the chiral CDs could reduce H2O2 damage, and display different reactive oxygen species scavenging activity. More significantly, to evaluate the universality of the strategy, the evolution of the chiral CDs preparation with adjusting the reaction conditions and replacing the achiral precursors was investigated. This research proposed a green and simple method for the preparation of chiral CDs and paves the way for the biological effects of chiral CDs.

Fructose, sucrose, lactose, ascorbic acid and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). D-cysteine and L-cysteine were purchased from Bidepharm Co., Ltd. (China). Other reagents are analytically pure and used as received. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water, which was purified by the Millipore water purification system (18.2 MΩ cm−1).

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of chiral L-/D-FruCDs were carried out on JEM-2100 high-resolution TEM (JEOL, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded on ESCALAB250Xi (Thermos, USA). Fourier Infrared spectra of L-/D-FruCDs were performed on FTIR5700 transform Spectrometer (Thermos, USA). The absorption and fluorescence spectra of L-/D-FruCDs were recorded by Cary series UV–vis absorption spectrometer and Cary fluorescence spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, USA).

The L-FruCDs was obtained as follows: firstly, 0.291 g L-cysteine and 0.036 g fructose were dissolved in 20 mL NaOH (0.4 mol/L) aqueous solution. Then, the mixture was transferred to the three-neck flask and heated to 80 °C in an oil bath with constant vigorous stirring for 3 h. During the reaction, the mixture turned from colorless and transparent to dark yellow with reaction time increasing, as a result of chiral CDs formation. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the reaction solution was directly dialysis with deionized water for 24 h to remove the impurities (MWCO: 1000 Da). Finally, the aqueous solution in the dialysis bag was lyophilized. To obtain D-FruCDs, D-cysteine was used in the process instead of L-cysteine according to the same method mentioned above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity analysis of L/D-FruCDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para:
The cytotoxicity of L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs were evaluated by 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl)− 2,5-diphenyltetrabromozole (MTT) assay. Human gastric cancer cells (SGC-7901) were cultured at 37 ℃ and 5% CO2 atmosphere. The SGC-7901 cells were inoculated into 96-well plates with the density of 2 × 103 cells/mL and incubated for 12 h to adhere. Then, the medium was replaced with fresh culture containing different concentrations and different types of chiral CDs. After the incubation of 24 h, the cytotoxicity of D-/L-FruCDs were evaluated according to the reported protocol[29]. All groups have been performed at least three times and recorded by Elx800 Micropalte reader (BioTek, USA). The cell viability was calculated using the following equation:
Cell viability (%) = (Abs treat -Abs zero) / (Abs ctrl -Abs zero) × 100%

Where Abs treat and Abs ctrl represent the absorption at 490 nm of the cells treated with or without chiral CDs. Abs zero represents the absorption at 490 nm of culture medium group, in which well there were no cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The abilities of L-/D-FruCDs for reducing oxidative damage
ce:section.ce:para: The SGC-7901 cells were inoculated into 96-well plates with the density of 2 × 104 cells/mL and incubated for 24 h to adhere. After that, the cells were firstly treated with 5 mM H2O2 for 2 h. Then, the medium was replaced by fresh culture medium containing different concentrations of L-FruCDs were used to incubate with cells for another 24 h. After incubation, the cell viability was measured via MTT assay as described in Section 2.3. To evaluate the protective effect of D-FruCDs for oxidative damage, D-FruCDs was used in the process instead of L-FruCDs according to the same method. All groups have been performed at least three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the SGC-7901 cells were seeded into 6-well plates for 24 h to adhere and then treated with 5 mM H2O2 for 2 h. After that, the culture medium was replaced by fresh culture medium containing 200 µg/mL L-FruCDs or D-FruCDs were added for another 4 h. Then the cells were treated with 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA) probe for another 15 min after washed with phosphate buffered solution (PBS) to evaluate the intracellular reactive oxygen species levels. After the collection and redispersion in PBS, the cells were analyzed using flow cytometry (Accuri C6, BD). All groups have been performed at least three times.
To validate the universality of the proposed method, different reaction conditions and reaction precursors were applied in the synthesis process.

Using the fructose and L-cysteine (or D-cysteine) precursors as described in Section 2.2, the solution with bright yellow fluorescence emission were obtained emission only by adjusting the concentration of NaOH aqueous solution to 0.1 mol/L and prolonging the reaction time to 24 h. After filtration, dialysis and freeze-drying, yellowish brown solid powder was obtained, which were named L-yFruCDs and D-yFruCDs, respectively. Next, by using the sucrose, lactose or ascorbic acid instead of the fructose precursor, and using the synthesis method mentioned above in Section 2.2, a series of other chiral CDs with different fluorescence emissions were also obtained.

Herein, we used fructose as carbon source, and chiral cysteine as chiral inducer to prepare chiral CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 1, L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs were synthesized by simple heating fructose and L-cysteine or D-cysteine aqueous solution in an oil bath under atmospheric pressure. It is worth noting that the reaction was conducted at relatively low temperature (80 °C). As we all know, the preparation of chiral carbon dots is strongly dependent on the chirality of precursor. However, the common hydrothermal method or pyrolysis method required high temperature (140–250 °C), which is harmful to the chiral source. Once the chirality of the precursor accidentally lost in the synthesized process, chiral CDs cannot be obtained [28]. Furthermore, Kang et al. reported that high reaction temperature (180 °C) resulted in CDs with inversed handedness compared with the chiral precursors [30]. Therefore, our low temperature reaction not only simplifies the preparation and purification process of chiral CDs compared with the “two-step” strategy, but also contributes to the chiral transference and biological activity of the precursors.

By controlling the concentration of NaOH at 0.4 mol/L and reaction time at 3 h in the synthesis process, two blue fluorescence-emitting chiral CDs were obtained, which named as L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs, respectively. The morphology and particle size distribution of chiral CDs were characterized by transmission electronic microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 2a and b, L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs are all spherical nanoparticles. The particle size distribution of L-FruCDs is ranging from 1.5 to 6.5 nm with an average size of 4.4 ± 0.9 nm (Fig. 2c). There was almost no lattice spacing in the high-resolution TEM image of L-FruCDs, indicating that the carbonic core of L-FruCDs display the amorphous nature. D-FruCDs has the similar morphology and structure to the L-FruCDs (Fig. 2b). The particle size distribution of D-FruCDs ranges from 2.0 to 10 nm with an average size of 5.6 ± 1.4 nm (Fig. 2d).

The surface functional groups and chemical composition of L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). It is worthing that L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs have the similar FTIR spectrum. As present in Fig. 2e, the broad and intense absorptions at 3480–3351 cm−1 and 3136–2992 cm−1 were induced by surface O-H and N-H stretching vibrations. The absorption peak at 2921 cm−1 was derived from asymmetric expansion vibration of C-H, and the peak at 1386 cm−1 was attributed to the bending vibration of C-H. The strong absorption peak at 1608 cm−1 was derived from the stretching of CO. In addition, there is a weak absorption peak at 2684–2508 cm−1, which corresponds to the S-H expansion vibration. The FTIR spectrum demonstrate that the surface of L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs mainly possessed some carboxyl, thiol and amido functional groups.

As shown in Fig. 2f (blue line), the full-scan X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of L-FruCDs showed five characteristic bond energy peaks of C1s (290.7 eV), O1s (534.3 eV), N1s (403.4 eV), S2s (227.3 eV), S2p (170.3 eV). This indicates that the chemical compositions of L-FruCDs were carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and sulfur elements, and the atomic percentages for the above elements were measured as 65.52%, 21.88%, 6.84%, 5.76%, respectively. The high resolution XPS of C1s spectrum was shown in Fig. S1 and it can be deconvolved into four peaks at 284.3 eV, 284.9 eV, 285.7 eV, and 287.9 eV, corresponding to the signals of CC (17.6%), C-C/C-N/C-S (36.5%), C-OH/CO (30.9%) and O-CO (15.0%), respectively (Table S1). Herein, the deconvolutions of peaks area were applied to simulate the proportion of different information of carbon element[31]. The high resolution XPS spectra of N1s could be divided into three peaks at 398.9 eV, 399.8 eV and 401.1 eV, indicating the formation of pyridinic nitrogen (53%), amino nitrogen (33%) and pyrrolic nitrogen (14%), respectively. The high resolution XPS spectra of O1s was fitted to 530.7 eV (COOH/CO) and 531.7 eV (C-O-C/C-OH). Additionally, the high-resolution S2p band obtained from L-FruCDs can be fitted into two primary peaks at 163.5 eV (S2p3/2) and 164.6 eV (S2p1/2), revealing the existence of -C-S- covalent bond of thiophene sulfur[19,31]. Similar, as illustrated in Fig. 2f (red line), the XPS spectra of D-FruCDs show four characteristic peaks at C1s (290.1 eV), O1s (534.1 eV), N1s (403.4 eV), S2p (170.3 eV), S2s (227.3 eV). And the atomic percentage of the elements was measured as carbon (67.29%), oxygen (21.01%), nitrogen (6.39%) and sulfur (5.31%). The high resolution XPS of C1s spectrum can be fitted into four peaks at 284.4 eV, 284.9 eV, 285.9 eV, 288.0 eV, corresponding to the signals of CC (26.8%), C-C/C-N/C-S (35.6%), C-OH/CO (23.7%) and O-CO (13.9%), respectively (Table S1). More information about the high resolution XPS spectra of D-FruCDs are shown in the supplementary material (Fig. S2).

As shown in Fig. 3a, the UV–vis absorption spectra of D-/L-FruCDs in aqueous solution were similar. Two weak absorption peaks at 249 nm and 300 nm were observed both in the spectra of L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs. The characteristic absorption peak at 249 nm could be assigned to π-π * transition of sp2 hybrid aromatic ring carbon structure, while the absorption peak at 300 nm corresponds to the n-π * transition of CO/CN/CS bonds, which mainly trapping the excited-state energy and contributing to the fluorescence of chiral CDs [22]. Interestingly, although their UV–vis absorption spectra are similar, the L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs exhibit an obvious preference absorption tendency towards the polarized light. As shown in Fig. 3b, circular dichroism spectra of L-FruCDs (red line) and D-FruCDs (blue line) present two symmetric and opposite absorption peaks at 215 nm and 252 nm, confirming the formation of chiral nanoparticles.

In this work, chiral CDs were prepared by heating the fructose and chiral cysteine molecules in the sodium hydroxide aqueous solution under atmospheric pressure. L-FruCDs was obtained from L-cysteine and D-FruCDs from D-cysteine. Thus, compared with chiral source (Fig. 3c), the chiral signal peak at 215 nm was held and directly transferred from molecular level to quantum level. In addition, different from chiral cysteine, there was a newly chiral peak at 252 nm observed for L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs, ruling out the direct contribution of the chiral precursors. Kumar etal. has synthesized chiral CDs using citric acid and chiral cysteine through hydrothermal method [26]. And they demonstrated that the circular dichroism signals of the obtained CDs showed a gradual increase first and then diminished with the increase of temperatures and the prolonging of reaction time. Thus, constant high temperature and long reaction time could increase the carbonization degree of the obtained nanoparticles, which leads to the breakdown of the chiral structure. As we know, the synthesis of CDs generally involves a series of condensation reactions, polymerization and carbonization process [19]. Therefore, the newly chiral signal peak at 252 nm of L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs may be assigned to the formation of π-π * conjugation between the chiral cysteine and fructose in the polymerized process. And the electronic transitions of aromatic ring systems near the stereocenters were affected by asymmetric groups, which generated newly chiral signal absorption [32]. These data indicated that the fluorescent CDs with inherent chirality were successfully prepared.

Fluorescence spectrum of chiral CDs was presented in Fig. 3d and e. Both L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs showed excitation-dependent emission behaviors. In other words, the maximum fluorescence emission shifted with the increasing of excitation. As for L-FruCDs, when the excitation wavelength increased from 320 nm to 440 nm, the fluorescence emission peak was red shifted from 435 nm to 512 nm. While under the same excitations, the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength of D-FruCDs was red shift from 440 nm to 519 nm. Strong emission peaks were observed at 443 nm and 439 nm upon excitation at 365 nm for L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs, respectively. The absolute quantum yields (QY) of prepared L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs were evaluated using FL 1000 Edinburgh Instruments spectrometer, and reached 4.0% and 1.7% respectively. The fluorescent lifetime curves of L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs fitted well with biexponential decay, which were shown in Fig. 3f and Table S2. Data indicate that the average lifetime of L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs were 2.74 ns and 3.02 ns under the excitation of 375 nm, respectively. Insert image of Fig. 3f shows both L-FruCDs (left) and D-FruCDs (right) emit bright blue fluorescence under the irradiation of ultraviolet lamp.

The stability of L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs at various pH and ionic strength have been studied to validate their applicability in biological systems. As shown in Fig. S3, chiral CDs are dissolved into Britton-Robinson buffer solution with different pH values. The fluorescent intensity of L-FruCDs increased rapidly as the pH value increases from 2 to 5, and remains stable from 6 to 10, but decreased in the strong alkali environment (11−13). D-FruCDs also show similar tendency, showing the excellent stability in the range of physiological pH values. Furthermore, ionic strength stability of chiral CDs is also evaluated. When the concentration of sodium chloride aqueous solutions increased from 0.2 to 2 mol/L, there is no noticeable change in fluorescent intensity of L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs. The above results demonstrate that L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs prepared in this work have excellent stability in the physiological pH values and high ionic strength (Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protect effect of L-/D-FruCDs against oxidative damage
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, human gastric cancer (SGC-7901) cells were used for cytotoxicity testing of chiral CDs by MTT assay. The cells were firstly incubated with various concentrations (50, 100, 200, 400 µg/mL) of L-FruCDs (or D-FruCDs) for 24 h. As shown in Fig. 4a, the cell viability of cells treated with L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs were both remained above 95% even at high concentration (400 µg/mL), indicating that the prepared chiral CDs exhibit negligible cytotoxicity. Additionally, it was observed that the cells survival rates for the L-FruCDs treated groups was slightly higher than that of D-FruCDs. As we all known, L-cysteine is an essential amino acid that makes up the organism, while D-cysteine is a synthetic amino acid [22]. Thus, the cytotoxicity of D-FruCDs derived from D-cysteine was slightly higher than that of L-FruCDs derived from L-cysteine. Furthermore, cell imaging results showed that both L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs did not induce significant damage to cell morphology even after long incubation for 24 h, although they can be easily internalized by SGC-7901 cells within 4 h and emit bright blue fluorescence in the cytoplasm (Fig. S4). The excellent biocompatibility gives the prepared chiral CDs the potential application for future biochemical application.

Reactive oxygen species (ROS) is essential in maintaining the intracellular redox homeostasis in biological systems. However, excess amounts of ROS cause severe oxidative stress, such as such as DNA damage, lipid peroxidation, or protein denaturation, resulting in cell apoptosis and necrosis [33,34]. According to the previous reports, CDs exhibit strong radical scavenging ability, which stem from the reducing ability of the structure and the electron donor from the dopant [35].

Thus, to properly investigate the ROS scavenging abilities of L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs, a series of experiments were carried out. Firstly, the protect effect of L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs against oxidative stress were measured. The SGC-7901 cells were treated with 5 mM H2O2 for 2 h, and then incubated with chiral CDs for another 24 h. After these treatments, the cell viability was evaluated. Compared to the negative control group, cell viability for H2O2-treated group decreased significantly, indicating H2O2 induced oxidative stress damage to cells (Fig. 4b). Interestingly, with the concentration of L-FruCDs increasing from 0 to 200 µg/mL, the cell viability increased from 55% to 63%. Meanwhile, when the concentration of D-FruCDs increased from 0 to 200 µg/mL, the cell viability increased from 55% to 60%. These results demonstrated that chiral CDs could protect cells from oxidative stress damage induced by H2O2 and the protective effect shows a significant concentration-dependent and chiral-dependent feature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular ROS-scavenging ability and the possible mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The following experiments were carried out to explain the possible mechanism of L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs for reducing oxidative damage. Non-fluorescent 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA) can be enzymatically hydrolyzed and oxidized into highly green fluorescent dichlorofluorescenin (DCF) by ROS [21]. In other words, the higher intracellular ROS content existed, the stronger fluorescence observed. Since the increase of fluorescence intensity of DCF in the presence of L-FruCDs or D-FruCDs were both small and closely to each other, the influence of chiral CDs on the fluorescence of DCF were neglected in the following studies (Fig. S5).

As shown in Figs. 4c and 4d, after stimulation of 5 mM H2O2 for 2 h, the bright green fluorescence intensity was obtained in the control group, indicating the upregulated expression of intracellular ROS. Meanwhile, compared with the control group, the green fluorescent intensities were significantly decreased to 67.7% in L-FruCDs group and 89.3% in D-FruCDs group. Namely, the reduction of fluorescence intensity in L-FruCDs group was almost 3-times than that of D-FruCDs group, which fully prove that L-FruCDs is superior than D-FruCDs to efficiently scavenging the intracellular ROS. In addition, the response of L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs to H2O2 were shown in Fig. S6. The fluorescence intensities of both L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs were maintained stable after incubating with 5 mM H2O2 for 15 min, indicating the chiral CDs cannot interact directly with H2O2 in aqueous solution (Fig. S6). Namely, the probably protect mechanism of L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs could be ascribed to the directly consumption the intracellular ROS.

As well all know, due to the experimental limitations on defining the exact structure and surface of CDs, it is difficult to investigate and explain the mechanism of CDs performance as ROS scavengers clearly. According to the previous reports, the ROS scavenging ability of carbon-based materials are influenced by the oxygen content, functional groups and element doping [34]. The oxygen content and oxidation degree are both related to the adduction formation, charge transfer and H donation ability. In general, oxygen atoms can introduce sp3 carbon skeleton and destroy the sp2 carbon domains, thereby decreasing the ROS-scavenging ability. However, once the oxygen atoms existed in the form of -OH, CO and COOH etc. oxygen containing functional groups, the ROS adsorption, adduction formation and electron transfer between ROS and carbon-based materials can be greatly improved. As shown in Table S1, it is obvious that the oxygen containing functional groups contents of C-OH/CO (30.9%) and O-C-O (15.0%) of L-FruCDs were both much higher than that of D-FruCDs (23.7% for C-OH/CO, 13.9% for O-CO). otherwise, the H donation and antioxidant performance of L-FruCDs were also promoted by its large amounts of -OH and -COOH.

Furthermore, the richer dopant contents of N (6.84%) and S (5.76%) elements can also facilitate the electron transfer between ROS and L-FruCDs than that of D-FruCDs (6.39% for N, 5.31% for S) [35]. In addition, the ROS scavenging of CDs may also be influenced by their chirality. As we all known, L-cysteine is an essential amino acid that makes up the organism, while D-cysteine is metabolite product. Therefore, the antioxidant activity of L-FruCDs derived from L-cysteine should be higher than that of D-FruCDs derived from D-cysteine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The universality test of the method
ce:section.ce:para: For any new method, it is important that the reaction parameters, such as reaction time, reaction precursors and rates be investigated in multiple assembly to evaluate the universality of the technique.

To verify the generality of this method, chiral CDs were synthesized by adjusting the reaction conditions firstly. As shown in Figs. 5a and 5b, when the concentration of NaOH decreased to 0.1 M and the reaction time prolonged to 24 h, CDs with both chirality and yellow fluorescence emission were obtained, which is completely different from the L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs. Herein, the new sets of prepared chiral CDs were named L-yFruCDs and D-yFruCDs, respectively. TEM images showed that the L-yFruCDs and D-yFruCDs exhibited smaller particle size and higher crystallinity of carbonic core (Fig. 5c and d). These results indicated that the pH values and reaction time in the synthesis process have strong influences on the structure of the produced CDs, and the chiral CDs prepared under the relatively mild conditions are more uniform. Moreover, compared with L-yFruCDs, a higher contrast and larger particle size was observed in D-yFruCDs. More information about the characterization of L-yFruCDs and D-yFruCDs are shown in Fig. S7.

In the following study, the fructose precursor was replaced with ascorbic acid, sucrose and lactose, respectively. When lactose and L-cysteine (or D-cysteine) as served as precursors, the L-LacCDs and D-LacCDs were obtained. As present in Fig. 6a, the obtained chiral L-/D-LacCDs showed strong polarized light select absorption within the range of 200 ∼ 300 nm. The maximum emissions peaks of the L-LacCDs and D-LacCDs were located at 519 nm and 512 nm (Fig. 6b and c). More information about the characterization of L-/D-AACDs and L-/D-SucCDs were shown in Fig. S8 and Fig.S9. The absolute fluorescent quantum yield and fitting data of fluorescence lifetime decay curves were shown in the Table S2. Representation data indicate that the fluorescence emission of these chiral CDs covers the ultraviolet, blue and green light regions (310 nm ∼ 520 nm). These results prove the universality of this synthetic method, which enrich the types of chiral CDs and paves the way for the biological effects studies of chiral CDs.

In summary, a facile strategy was adopted for the synthesis of chiral CDs in this work. Namely, the reaction of the chiral cysteine precursors with fructose in the sodium hydroxide aqueous solution under atmospheric pressure resulted in the formation of L-FruCDs and D-FruCDs. The synthesized chiral CDs exhibited different ability for reducing oxidative stress. Moreover, the newly synthesis method is scalable by replacing fructose with ascorbic acid, sucrose and lactose. The demonstration of polarized light selectivity of chiral CDs coupled with their other advantages, such as good water solubility, excellent light stability, low toxicity, makes them excellent candidates for the applications in chiral enantiomer recognition, biological activity regulation and disease treatment.

Wang Yuying: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Zhang Xiaoyang: Formal analysis, Investigation. Wang Beibei: Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Liu Yi: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by GDAS’ Project of Science and Technology Development (2021GDASYL-20210103010A), and National Natural Science Foundation of China (U23A2089).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2024.113784.
